beats.narration_text: Olá — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to make an animated GIF for Slack. The Slack GIF Creator fires. It knows Slack's exact specs: emoji GIFs at 128 by 128 pixels, message GIFs at 480, FPS between 10 and 30, colors between 48 and 128. It has three utilities: GIFBuilder for assembling and optimizing frames, validators to check Slack compliance, and easing functions for smooth motion. The animation logic itself uses PIL ImageDraw primitives — the skill gives you knowledge and tools, not rigid templates. Let me show you the spec, the toolkit, and the animation concepts.

props.command: Make me a bouncing star emoji GIF for Slack — something eye-catching for our team channel.
output: Animation: bounce + pulse combo using ease_out + sin wave

beats.narration_text: The skill separates into two concerns: the spec and the toolkit. Spec first. There are two format tracks. Emoji GIFs: 128 by 128 pixels, 10 to 30 FPS, 48 to 128 colors, and keep them under 3 seconds. Message GIFs: 480 by 480 pixels, same FPS and color range. Three utilities abstract the hard parts. GIFBuilder takes width, height, and FPS, accepts PIL Image frames, and saves with color quantization — set num_colors to 48 and optimize_for_emoji to True for emoji mode. Validators let you check compliance before delivery: validate_gif for a detailed report, is_slack_ready for a quick boolean. The easing module gives you seven motion curves — linear, ease_in, ease_out, ease_in_out, bounce_out, elastic_out, and back_out. Pass a progress value from 0 to 1 and get a smoothed value back. Dependencies: Pillow, imageio, numpy.

props.sparkLine: Know the spec. Use the toolkit. Write the PIL logic yourself.

beats.narration_text: The skill documents eight animation concepts. Shake: offset position with math.sin using the frame index, add small random variations for a natural feel. Pulse or heartbeat: scale size rhythmically with sin — two quick pulses then pause for the heartbeat variant. Bounce: use ease_in for falling, bounce_out for landing. Spin or rotate: PIL's rotate method, or a sine wave for wobble. Fade in and out: RGBA image, adjust alpha, or Image.blend between two frames. Slide: start off-screen, interpolate with ease_out for a smooth stop, back_out for an overshoot. Zoom: scale from 0.1 to 2.0 and crop the center. Explode or particle burst: generate particles with random angles and velocities, apply gravity by incrementing the y velocity each frame, and fade out alpha over time. The explicit message: combine concepts — bouncing plus rotating, pulsing plus sliding. Use PIL's full capabilities.

props.sparkLine: Combine concepts. PIL does more than you think.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The architectural insight of the Slack GIF Creator is that it solves a constraint problem, not a creativity problem: the spec is fixed, compliance is verifiable, and the toolkit handles the tedious parts so the animation logic can be expressive. What it gets right: exact Slack specs are baked in and the validator catches non-compliant output before delivery; GIFBuilder abstracts frame assembly and handles color quantization automatically; seven easing functions cover the full range of smooth motion; and the explicit no-rigid-templates philosophy gives full PIL access for creative work. Where it bites: PIL-only drawing makes complex shapes like hearts and snowflakes require careful polygon math by hand; there is no text rendering guidance since emoji fonts are flagged as unreliable and no alternative is given; color palette dithering artifacts at 48 colors are not addressed; seamless looping requires matching first and last frames manually with no helper for it; and the three dependencies must be pre-installed — the skill does not install them.

props.sparkLine: GIF is a constraint problem. Solve it with PIL primitives.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The Slack GIF Creator bakes in Slack's exact specs: emoji at 128 by 128 pixels, under 3 seconds, message at 480 by 480. Two utilities to know: GIFBuilder for frame assembly and color optimization, and the validator to check compliance before delivery. Seven easing functions for smooth motion. Eight animation concepts in the skill: shake, pulse, bounce, spin, fade, slide, zoom, explode. Philosophy: provide knowledge and utilities, not rigid templates — write the animation logic with PIL ImageDraw primitives directly. Always validate before delivery.

artifactLines: Message: 480×480, same FPS (10–30) and color range
GIFBuilder: frame assembly + color quantization + emoji optimization
Validator: validate_gif() / is_slack_ready() — check before delivery
7 easing curves: linear, ease_in/out, bounce_out, elastic_out, back_out
8 animation concepts: shake, pulse, bounce, spin, fade, slide, zoom, explode

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude session. Paste this: Make me a pulsing fire emoji GIF for Slack. It should loop smoothly, feel energetic, and stay under 3 seconds. Use the Slack GIF Creator skill. Then watch three things: first, does Claude look up the emoji spec — 128 by 128 pixels, under 3 seconds, 48 to 128 colors — before writing any code? Second, does it use GIFBuilder and the easing module rather than writing its own frame assembly from scratch? And third, does it call is_slack_ready or validate_gif on the output before declaring it done? If Claude generates a GIF without checking the validator, it may be delivering a file Slack will reject. And if it writes its own animation loop from scratch without using the easing functions, it reinvented something the skill already provides. Those are your gates.

props.command: Make me a pulsing fire emoji GIF for Slack. Smooth loop, energetic, under 3 seconds. Use the Slack GIF Creator skill.
props.runningText: Watch: does Claude check emoji spec? Use GIFBuilder + easing? Run validator before delivery?
output: Expected: emoji spec looked up (128×128, ≤3s) before any code
Expected: GIFBuilder + easing module used, not reinvented
Red flag: no validator call before declaring the GIF done

beats.narration_text: Slack GIF Creator. Liam, in for Bear.
